retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Pathophysiology -- Congenital Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Megakaryopoiesis initiates from the hematopoietic stem cell within the bone marrow. The hematopoietic stem cell undergoes maturation into a multipotent progenitor cell, transitioning into a committed megakaryocyte progenitor cell, then an immature megakaryocyte, and ultimately matures into a fully developed megakaryocyte, responsible for generating platelets in the blood. [29] Notably, THPO acts as a crucial cytokine during the process of megakaryopoiesis, facilitating the proliferation, enlargement, and polyploidy of megakaryocytes while stimulating the expression of platelet-specific markers. [4] In addition, THPO boosts the expression of vascular endothelial growth factor, homeobox B4 ( HOXB4) , and homeobox A9 ( HOXA9 ), thereby supporting the growth and survival of hematopoietic stem cells. [10] Although the liver primarily synthesizes THPO, the body can also produce some in the proximal tubular cells of the kidneys and bone marrow stromal cells. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hematopoietic pluripotent stem cells have self-renewal capability and give rise to either myeloid or the lymphoid lineage which further differentiates into various mature blood cells such as red blood cells (RBC), lymphocytes, granulocytes, megakaryocytes, and macrophages. The hematopoietic process is determined by the bone marrow environment, growth factors, and transcription factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Hematologic Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: About 10% of patients with chronic myeloid leukemia (TML) develop leukemogenesis of acute megakaryoblastic leukemia (AMKL) before the age of 4 years. AMKL is associated with GATA1 gene which is an X-linked transcriptor factor leading to an uncontrolled proliferation of immature megakaryocytes. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Pathophysiology -- Acquired Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Antibody or T-cell–mediated autoimmunity may cause acquired amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia. [21] The proposed pathophysiology includes antibodies targeted against THPO, antigens on megakaryocyte progenitor cells, granulocyte-monocyte colony-stimulating factor, or the megakaryocyte colony-forming unit. Failure of terminal megakaryocyte differentiation, suppression of megakaryocyte colony-forming unit by T cells and adherent monocytes, monoclonal T-cell population destroying megakaryocyte lineage, or a defect in cytokine-mediated regulation of megakaryopoiesis are additional proposed mechanisms. [6] [22] [25] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Etiology -- Congenital Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Although a biallelic variation in c-MPL occurs in most patients with congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia-2, additional genetic variations are possible. A homozygous variation of THPO (OMIM 600044.0011) prevents liver cells from producing THPO. [13] [14] According to Thompson and Nguyen, abnormalities in the homeobox A11 ( HOXA11 )  and  MDS1 and EVI1 complex locus ( MECOM )  genes lead to congenital amegakaryocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and eventual bone marrow failure in most patients. [3] [15] Clinicians differentiate patients with the HOXA11 and MECOM genetic variations from other forms of congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia due to their association with radioulnar synostosis. MECOM-associated syndrome is an autosomal dominant form of congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia, also presenting with bone marrow failure, radioulnar synostosis, B-cell deficiency, and cardiorenal dysfunction. [16] Mutations in this gene affect factor EVI1, which controls MPL transcription. In addition, a null mutation in RBM8A leads to thrombocytopenia-absent radius syndrome, which is differentiated from other forms of congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia by the presence of bilateral radial aplasia. Additional proposed alternate etiologies include an X-linked variety of congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia and an anti-human leukocyte antigen (HLA) A2 antibody. [10] [13] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Marrow Failure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia is not associated with chromosome fragility; however, mutations in MPL (myeloproliferative leukemia virus) oncogene lead to thrombocytopenia from absent or reduced megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. MPL is responsible for coding thrombopoietin receptor c-MPL, a regulator of megakaryocytopoiesis and platelet production. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry of Platelet Activating Factor -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: PAFR transcript-1 is mostly involved in inflammation and pathological processes and is synthesized in response to stimulation by PAF in a positive feedback manner through the activation of NF-kB. PAFR transcript-2 is controlled by various hormones and cytokines such as estrogen, TGF-beta, retinoic acid, or T3. Retinoic acid and thyroid hormone are potent inducers of PAFR transcript-2. Estrogen also increases PAFR transcript-2 levels, and TFG-beta decreases its levels. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Structure -- Regulation
retrieved_docs.content: Our understanding of the transcription factors and growth factors that govern hematopoiesis has continued to demonstrate the complexity of the system. Particular interest has been paid in determining the genes and factors that influence a malignant transformation. Selected examples of known important transcription factors include:

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: Megakaryocyte precursors are difficult to distinguish microscopically from myeloblasts. Further along in differentiation, they gain their distinctive cytoplasmic granulation and numerous nuclear lobes.

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia is a severe form of thrombocytopenia characterized by diminished or absent megakaryocytes in the bone marrow without other abnormalities. This condition manifests in both congenital and acquired forms. Congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia manifests with severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding issues at birth, often progressing to bone marrow failure, whereas acquired amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia typically emerges later in life and may be linked to immune-mediated mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Pathophysiology -- Congenital Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Located in the bone marrow, liver, spleen, and on CD34+ cells, THPO binding initiates a cascade of signaling events within the target cell through the Janus kinase/signal transducers and activators of transcription  kinase proteins, mitogen-activated protein kinase, and phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase pathways. [4] [30] MPL signaling is vital for differentiating multipotent progenitor cells into megakaryocyte and erythrocyte progenitor cells. Problems in signaling can lead to impaired megakaryocyte and erythrocyte production, as noted in the later stages of congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia. [31] Notably, decreased expression of P-selectin on neonatal platelets can reduce platelet activation and predispose affected infants to the high bleeding tendency pre- or perinatally in patients with congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hematopoietic pluripotent stem cells have self-renewal capability and give rise to either myeloid or the lymphoid lineage which further differentiates into various mature blood cells such as red blood cells (RBC), lymphocytes, granulocytes, megakaryocytes, and macrophages. The hematopoietic process is determined by the bone marrow environment, growth factors, and transcription factors. The abnormal proliferation of one or more terminal myeloid cell lines in the peripheral blood gives rise to a heterogeneous group of disorders called myeloproliferative neoplasms. Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), polycythemia vera (PV), essential thrombocythemia (ET), and primary myelofibrosis (PMF) are four classic types of myeloproliferative neoplasms. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of myeloproliferative neoplasms and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hemostasis -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Platelets . These are non-nucleated disc-like cells created from megakaryocytes that arise from the bone marrow. They are about 2 to 3 microns in size. Some of their unique structural elements include plasma membrane, open canalicular system, spectrin and actin cytoskeleton, microtubules, mitochondria, lysosomes, granules, and peroxisomes. [5] These cells release proteins involved in clotting and platelet aggregation. Hepatocytes . The liver produces the majority of the proteins that function as clotting factors and as anticoagulants.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Bone Marrow -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Cellular distribution
retrieved_docs.content: The distribution of maturing hematopoietic elements is better discerned in trephine biopsy sections. Erythroid cells typically cluster in the interstitium. Meanwhile, immature myeloid precursors reside in the paratrabecular spaces, progressing toward the interstitium as they mature. Megakaryocytes normally have a perisinusoidal distribution. Paratrabecular megakaryocyte growth signifies abnormal hematopoiesis. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombopoietin Receptor Agonists -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to their ability to increase platelet counts by stimulating thrombopoietin receptors and inducing gene transcription, eltrombopag and romiplostim demonstrate immunoregulatory properties, leading to heightened levels of regulatory T cells in individuals with ITP. This particular effect is facilitated by transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β), which is notably abundant in megakaryocytes and platelets. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Two genes, FLI1 and ETS1 , are suspected to contribute to key symptoms of Jacobsen syndrome. Both are located on the long arm of chromosome 11. Loss of FLI1 and ETS1 has been linked to thrombocytopenia and cardiac defects, respectively. The loss of the transcription factor FLI1 leads to Paris-Trousseau syndrome and thrombocytopenia due to impaired hematopoiesis and megakaryocyte differentiation. [10] [11] ETS1 is a transcription factor vital for angiogenesis and cardiac development. The loss of ETS1 leads to abnormal myocardial and coronary artery development in animal models. [12] The pathophysiology of immunodeficiency remains unclear; however, case reports indicate low immunoglobulin G levels, a decreased humoral response to pneumococcal vaccination, and reduced T-cell and natural killer cell numbers. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII, also referred to as faktor VIII, is used as a therapeutic agent for coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders. By supplying the deficient clotting protein, factor VIII, human helps restore normal hemostasis and control bleeding in patients affected by these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2058
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII is used as a therapeutic agent to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, helping to correct deficient clotting activity and control bleeding. By supplementing the missing or insufficient factor VIII, it restores normal hemostasis in conditions such as hemophilia A and other bleeding disorders, thereby reducing the risk of severe hemorrhage and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2201
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VII is classified as a biologically active substance, meaning it belongs to the broader group of compounds that exert physiological effects in the body. Recognizing Factor VII as a biologically active substance highlights its role in essential processes such as the initiation of the coagulation cascade, where it contributes to blood clot formation and hemostasis. This classification aids clinicians and researchers in understanding the functional importance of Factor VII within the complex network of biologically active agents that regulate normal vascular and hemostatic function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K and other hemostatics produce hemostatické účinky, meaning they exert the physiological hemostatic effects that stop bleeding by promoting clot formation and maintaining vascular integrity. These hemostatic actions are central to the therapeutic role of vitamin K and related agents in controlling hemorrhage and supporting normal coagulation processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory enzymes have a broader relationship with protein C, indicating that protein C is subject to regulation by a variety of inhibitory enzymatic mechanisms. This broad interaction underscores the importance of multiple inhibitory pathways in modulating protein C activity, which is central to the control of coagulation and may inform therapeutic strategies targeting hemostatic balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes, hematological are identified as members of both the deoxyribonuclease group and the trypsin group, indicating that these blood‑related enzymes are classified within two distinct enzyme families. As members of the deoxyribonuclease family, they possess nuclease activity capable of cleaving DNA, while their classification as members of the trypsin family reflects proteolytic functions typical of serine proteases. This dual categorization highlights the multifunctional nature of hematological enzymes, which can participate in both nucleic acid metabolism and protein digestion processes within the circulatory system, underscoring the importance of recognizing overlapping enzymatic roles in patient care and biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: positivity intersect with these gastrointestinal pathologies. Recognizing these interconnections supports comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of patients presenting with overlapping gastrointestinal and hematologic findings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglobulin, blood coagulation factors, and protein hydrolytic enzymes are all entities for which ultrastructural characteristics are recognized as valid descriptors; the ultrastructure of each of these proteins can be examined to elucidate their detailed molecular organization and functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1636
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities (Gestation abnormality) are linked as a downstream condition of bleeding in pregnancy, indicating that hemorrhagic events can contribute to broader developmental disturbances of the pregnancy. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how various placental positions, coagulation abnormalities, timing of hemorrhage, and associated hematologic findings converge under the single clinical concept of bleeding in pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing each subtype for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2714
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is classified as a member of the hematological enzymes group, indicating its role in blood‑related enzymatic processes, and it functions as the active ingredient of the therapeutic agent Fibrinolysin, human.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2296
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics are a class of medications that inhibit the enzymatic breakdown of fibrin clots, thereby promoting hemostasis in situations of excessive bleeding. Vitamin K 1 (phylloquinone) and vitamin K 2 (menaquinone) are distinct forms of the vitamin K family that serve as essential cofactors for the γ‑carboxylation of several clotting factors, a process required for normal coagulation. Although both antifibrinolytics and the vitamin K compounds influence the coagulation system, antifibrinolytics are not subtypes of vitamin K 1 or vitamin K 2; they act through a different mechanism by directly blocking plasminogen activation rather than by providing the vitamin K‑dependent enzymatic support needed for clot factor maturation. Recognizing this distinction is important for patient care, as antifibrinolytic agents such as tranexamic acid are used to control acute hemorrhage, whereas vitamin K 1 or vitamin K 2 supplementation is employed to correct vitamin K deficiency–related bleeding disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “cancer; other and unspecified primary” includes malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) as a member of this group, indicating that these hematologic malignancies are categorized under cancers of other or unspecified primary origin.
